next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

The Hellas basin opened under the Bebop like somebody had hit Mars with a bottle and left the glass where it fell.

Red canyons split the ground in long crooked seams. Shadow sat in them thick and blue-black. On the rims, settlements clung to the rock in little rusted clusters of tanks, sheds, antenna poles, and dock lights that blinked through the dust as if they were surprised to still have power. The sky above all of it had that thin bad color Mars did at the end of the day, peach where the light caught and sick brass where it did not.

Jet hated chasing anything down here in a ship this size.

He sat forward in the pilot's chair with his good hand on the throttle and his metal one wrapped around the yoke, feeling every complaint the Bebop made through the frame. The trawler was built to endure, not to dance. She could take damage and keep flying. She could haul mass. She could drift for days. What she could not do was snake through smugglers' cuts in the southern basin after a custom ship that weighed maybe a quarter of what she did and moved like it had no furniture inside.

On the monitor over Ed's head the target ping slipped between canyon returns, reappeared, slipped again.

Ed had one foot on the console and the other up against the back of the chair, folded wrong in a way that made Jet's shoulder ache just looking at it. Ein stood with his front paws on the edge of the station, ears up, barking every time the ping jumped.

"Ed has the sneaky fish," Ed said. "Sneaky fish is going south-southish and then a little bite bite to the left. No, the other le...
open_hooks_to_carry: Spike checks dead drops and safe houses — most destroyed, one intact with a moved cargo manifest annotated 'She needed this to live'
The manifest is for immunosuppressant drugs routed through Spike's logistics cover as part of a weapons shipment
prev_tail_excerpt:
..., eyes losing the room and coming back sharper. Spike's attention snagged every few minutes on the dark outside the porthole, on nothing Jet could see. Ed was noisier than usual, which with Ed was sometimes joy and sometimes weather cover. Jet himself had spent the day walking through a name he'd filed away under shame and practicality and youth, only to learn a dead man had better access to it than he did.

Small ship. Thin walls. No privacy worth the word.

They still managed to build it, piece by piece, out of timing and omissions and the mercy of not asking the next question.

Faye set her empty bowl aside and said, "This would've been better with mushrooms."

"Everything's better with mushrooms."

"That's the smartest thing you've said all day."

Jet gathered bowls for the sink. Water knocked in the pipes when he turned the tap. Behind him Ed started explaining something to Ein about recursive ghosts in a public records cache. Spike finally lit the cigarette and cracked the vent. Cold air threaded in around the smoke, carrying dock-lot dust and the faint metallic smell of evening.

Jet looked over his shoulder once.

Spike was watching the smoke get pulled toward the vent, face unreadable. Faye had her chin in her hand, staring at nothing. Ed was talking with both hands now, tape still bright on the big toe, as if the room hadn't just tightened and loosened around them in ways a kid would ignore or understand too well. The Bebop ticked softly in the cold at the seams.

On a ship this small, silence was rarely empty. Mostly it was built. Tonight they built plenty of it.
state_deltas_required: Spike's alias reactivation is a genuine syndicate-world threat — successor organizations still monitor old networks

The investigation becomes active pursuit; Spike's discovery of the annotated manifest suggests the pilot has a personal connection to the syndicate supply chain; the crew unites in pursuit for four different reasons
